narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into building a strong foundation with your impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical student's 3.21—and a solid LSAT score of 37, right around the average of 36.70. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, your Year 1 school decile of 1 and Year 3 decile of 1, both significantly lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, stood out in the admission model's evaluation. This model, which looks at past students' profiles to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated your probability of failing at 54.7%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes those predicted to pass. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile rank, which had the strongest negative influence compared to others. Your Year 1 decile rank came next, also contributing substantially toward the fail prediction and outweighing some of your strengths. On a brighter note, your high undergraduate GPA played the most significant role in pushing the prediction toward passing, closely followed by your LSAT score, which helped counterbalance the concerns. Your family income and full-time status offered minor positive nudges but weren't decisive. Compared to typical students, your early academic deciles are in the bottom tier, which amplified the model's worry despite your later college achievements. We know this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in competitive fields, but it's not the end of your journey. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, gathering stronger recommendation letters highlighting your resilience, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background—your GPA and LSAT give you a great shot with some targeted improvements. Keep pushing; the legal world needs your voice.
